required.title: [For Student] A progress chart or log

supplementary.title: [For Student — Read Aloud] Stories of builders, growers, and makers

competence_markers.description: They work on a project across multiple days without needing to be convinced to return to it each time
They track their own progress using a chart, notebook, or other visible method — and they reference it
They complete at least one long-term project (minimum two weeks of regular work) fully, without abandoning it for a new project
They can describe what they have done so far on a project and what remains — they have a sense of the arc
They push through the middle of a project — the phase where excitement has faded — without requiring extraordinary intervention
They do not replace an unfinished project with a new one when interest declines
They participate in a completion ritual — they mark the end of a finished project as an event worth recognizing
They can articulate, in their own words, that effort accumulates — that today's work builds on yesterday's

narration_prompts.detail: What is it? How long have you been working on it? What does it look like right now compared to when you started?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the hardest part of working on something for a long time?
narration_prompts.detail: Not the hardest part of the project itself — the hardest part of keeping going.

narration_prompts.prompt: Have you ever wanted to quit your project and start something new?
narration_prompts.detail: What happened? What did you do?
What does it tell you about the work you have done? Can you see the days adding up?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about something you finished that took a long time.
narration_prompts.detail: How did you feel when it was done? Was it worth the effort?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about someone from a story who kept going when it was hard.
narration_prompts.detail: What made them keep going? What would have happened if they had stopped?

cross_connections.description: The daily structure that makes regular project work sessions possible. Without IC2.1's rhythms, the project sessions will not happen consistently enough to build the accumulation IC2.4 depends on.

Finishing is not enough — the work must meet a standard. IC2.2's "do it right" principle prevents the student from rushing through the last phase of a project just to be done. Completion at Stretching Branches means complete AND done properly.
Each work session on the long-term project is a focus session. IC2.3's single-tasking and completion-before-switching principles are practiced every time the student sits down to work on the project.
Care for tools, respect for materials, and pride in honest work all apply directly to the long-term project. The finished product is the first artifact of craftsmanship the student produces.

The operating system on which everything runs. The daily discipline, task completion, and self-governance built in IC1.1 are what make sustained multi-day effort possible. Without those foundations, the student will not persist through the middle.

The feelings that arise in the middle of a long project — boredom, frustration, the desire to quit — are the emotional terrain IC3.1 helps the student navigate. The two sub-domains reinforce each other directly.

Many of the best Stretching Branches long-term projects are building or making projects that connect directly to early trades exposure. Building a birdhouse, growing a garden, and assembling a model are all entry points into the craftsmanship domains.

content.why_this_matters:
Some things cannot be finished in one day. A garden does not grow in an afternoon. A model ship is not built in a single sitting. A drawing that fills an entire page takes more than one session at the table.

This packet is about learning something that sounds simple but changes everything: **effort accumulates.** What you do today adds to what you did yesterday. What you do tomorrow will add to what you did today. And if you keep going — if you come back and do more, even when you do not feel like it — something real gets built. Something you could not have made in one try.

Most people never learn this. They start things and stop. They get excited about a project on Monday and forget about it by Wednesday. They want to see the finished thing right now, and when they cannot have it right now, they quit and start something new. Then they quit that too. They end up with a pile of half-started things and nothing finished.

You are going to be different. You are going to learn what it feels like to come back to something, to keep working on it, and to finish it. And when you finish it, you will know something about yourself that most people never discover: **you are the kind of person who finishes what they start.**
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Multi-day effort** — the experience of working on something across several days or weeks, returning to it each time and picking up where you left off
2. **Progress tracking** — seeing that what you did yesterday is still there, and that today's work adds to it
3. **Completion** — finishing a project, all the way, before starting something new
4. **Patience with process** — learning that not everything worth having arrives quickly
5. **The habit of returning** — coming back to a task that is not finished, even when something new sounds more exciting
6. **Pride in accumulated work** — the specific feeling of looking at something you built over many days and knowing that every piece of it came from your effort
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Some Things Take More Than One Day

You already know how to finish a task. You clean up your blocks. You make your bed. You complete your chore. Those are things you start and finish in the same sitting.

But some things are too big to finish in one sitting. Think about a garden. You cannot plant a seed and have a tomato the same afternoon. The seed needs time. It needs water — not once, but every day. It needs sun. It needs you to come back, again and again, and take care of it. And then, weeks later, something grows that you could not have made in a day no matter how hard you tried.

That is what a long-term project is. It is something you work on over many days — sometimes many weeks. You do a little bit each time. And each time you come back, you will see that what you did before is still there, waiting for you to add more.

**Here is what makes this hard:** After the first day or two, the excitement fades. The project is not new anymore. It is not finished yet. It is just... in the middle. The middle is where most people quit. They want to feel the excitement of starting something new, so they abandon what they already began.

You are learning not to do that. You are learning to stay in the middle, to keep working, and to trust that the finish will come if you keep going.

### Part 2: Choosing Your Project

Your first long-term project should have three qualities:

**1. It must be visible.** You need to see the progress. A garden grows taller. A model gets more pieces. A drawing gets more detail. A building project gets bigger. If you cannot see that today's work added something to yesterday's work, the project is not right for this stage.

**2. It must take at least two weeks.** If you can finish it in two days, it is not a long-term project. It is just a task. The point is to experience the sustained effort — the coming back, the continuing, the working through the middle.

**3. It must be something real.** Not a worksheet. Not a screen activity. Something physical that exists in the world when you are done — something you can hold, point to, walk through, or harvest.

**Examples of good first projects:**

- **A garden:** Plant seeds. Water them every day. Watch them grow. Weed around them. Harvest what grows. This is one of the best long-term projects because the timeline is built into nature — you cannot rush a plant, and the plant rewards consistent daily care.
- **A model or building project:** A model ship, a birdhouse, a wooden shelf, a fort. Something that requires multiple work sessions — measuring, cutting, assembling, finishing. Each session adds visible progress.
- **A large drawing or art project:** A mural for your wall. A book of drawings — one per day for a month. A detailed scene that you add to each day. The accumulation is visible every time you look at it.
- **A collection with organization:** Collect rocks, leaves, insects, or flowers. Identify them. Label them. Build a display. This combines daily effort (collecting) with long-term organization (identifying and displaying).

Your mentor will help you choose. But the choice should be something you genuinely care about — because caring is what will get you through the middle.

### Part 3: The Work Session

A work session is the time you spend on your project each day — or on whatever schedule you and your mentor decide. It does not have to be long. For younger students, 15 to 20 minutes is enough. For older Stretching Branches students, 20 to 45 minutes. The important thing is not how long each session is, but that the sessions happen consistently.

**Before you begin each session:**
- Look at where you left off last time. What did you do? What is next?
- Gather your materials. Do not start until you have what you need.
- Decide what you will do in this session. Not the whole project — just today's piece.

**During the session:**
- Work on the project. Not on something else. Not half on this and half on that. This is the time for this project.
- If you get stuck, ask for help or try a different approach. Getting stuck is not a reason to quit.
- If you get bored, keep working. Boredom in the middle of a project is normal. It is not a signal that you should stop. It is a signal that you are in the middle — and the middle is exactly where the important learning happens.

**After the session:**
- Clean up your workspace and put your materials away properly.
- Mark your progress. On your chart, in your log, on your calendar — record what you did today.
- Look at what you have built so far. See the accumulation.

### Part 4: The Progress Chart

Your progress chart is how you see the accumulation. It makes the invisible visible. Without it, all you see is today's work. With it, you see every day's work stacked together.

**Ways to track progress:**

- **A wall chart:** A piece of paper on the wall near where you work. Each day you work on the project, you add a mark, a sticker, or a note about what you did. Over time, the chart fills up — and filling up IS the progress.
- **A project notebook:** A small notebook where you write or draw one thing about each work session. "Today I planted three rows of seeds." "Today I glued the hull pieces together." "Today I drew the mountains in the background." When you flip through the pages, you see the whole story of the project.
- **A photo log:** Take a photo of the project after each session. Line up the photos. Watch it grow.
- **A calendar:** Mark each day you worked on the project. The unbroken (or mostly unbroken) chain of marks is itself a measure of perseverance.

The chart is not optional. It is part of the practice. The reason is simple: young minds can forget how much work they have already done. On a hard day in the middle, when the project feels like it will never be finished, looking at the chart reminds you: *I have already done this much. The work is real. The finish is getting closer.*

### Part 5: The Middle

The middle is the hardest part of any long-term project. It is where you are past the excitement of beginning but not yet close to the satisfaction of finishing. The middle is where the project just feels like... work.

**What happens in the middle:**
- You might want to start something new instead. A new project sounds exciting. This one does not, anymore.
- You might feel like it will never be finished. The end seems far away.
- You might feel like your work is not very good. Yesterday's effort does not look as impressive today.
- You might just not feel like working on it. Not for any particular reason — you just do not want to.

**All of these feelings are normal.** Every person who has ever finished anything important has felt every one of them. The gardener gets tired of watering. The builder gets tired of sanding. The artist gets tired of drawing the same scene. The feelings are real — but they are not instructions. They are not telling you to stop. They are telling you that you are in the middle.

**What you do in the middle:** You work anyway. Not because you feel like it. Not because it is exciting. Because this is what you committed to, and you are the kind of person who finishes what they start.

Your mentor will help you through the middle. They will not rescue you from it — because being rescued from the middle teaches you nothing. But they will be there, reminding you that the middle always ends, and that what comes after the middle is the finish.

### Part 6: No Replacing Unfinished With New

This is a rule, not a suggestion: **you do not start a new long-term project until you finish the one you are working on.**

The temptation will come. You will see something — a new kit, a new idea, a friend's project — and you will want to abandon what you are doing and start that instead. The new thing has the excitement of beginning. The current thing has the weight of the middle.

If you give in to that temptation, you learn something destructive: quitting is how you escape difficulty. And you will carry that lesson into everything. New job when the current one gets hard. New relationship when the current one requires effort. New goal when the old one demands perseverance. The pattern starts here, with small projects, and it scales into a life of abandoned things.

So the rule holds. Finish first. Then begin something new. The new project will still be there when you are done.

**One exception:** If a project turns out to be genuinely wrong — the materials failed, the plan was impossible, the scope was far beyond what anyone could do — your mentor may help you adjust the project or, in rare cases, help you end it honestly. But "I got bored" is not a reason to stop. "I want to do something else" is not a reason to stop. Those are reasons to keep going.

### Part 7: Finishing and the Completion Ritual

Finishing matters. Not just because the project is done — but because of what finishing teaches you about yourself.

When you finish a long-term project, you should mark it. This is what we call a **completion ritual** — a way of recognizing that something significant happened. You committed to something, you worked through the middle, and you finished.

**What a completion ritual might look like:**

- **Show your finished project** to your family. Walk them through it. Tell them what you did, how long it took, what was hard, and what you are most proud of.
- **Put it on display.** If it is a model, find a place for it. If it is a garden, harvest it and prepare a meal with what you grew. If it is a drawing, frame it or hang it. The finished product deserves a place of honor — not because it is perfect, but because it is complete.
- **Record it in your progress log.** The final entry: "Finished." Write the date. This is the record of a promise kept.
- **Celebrate.** This does not need to be elaborate. A special dessert, a trip to a favorite place, an extra hour of free time. The celebration is not for the product — it is for the perseverance.

After the completion ritual, take some time before starting the next long-term project. Let the satisfaction of finishing settle. Think about what you want to do next. And when you begin the next project, you will begin it as someone who has finished something — which is a fundamentally different person than someone who has not.
content.formative_practices:
These are the ongoing practices that build IC2.4 capacity at Stretching Branches. They are not one-time activities — they are habits and rhythms that develop perseverance over months.

### Practice 1: The Long-Term Project [Ongoing — Minimum 2 Weeks per Project]
Choose, execute, and complete a long-term project as described in the Core Content. Work on it regularly — daily if possible, or on a set schedule of at least three sessions per week. The project must be visible, physical, and require sustained effort across many sessions. When one project is complete, after a rest period, begin another. Over the course of Stretching Branches, the student should complete at least three to four long-term projects of increasing scope.

### Practice 2: Daily Progress Marking [After Each Work Session]
After every work session on the long-term project, mark your progress. Use whatever tracking method you have chosen — chart, notebook, photos, calendar. This is not optional. The marking is part of the practice. It trains the habit of noticing accumulation and builds the evidence you will need when the middle gets hard.

### Practice 3: The "Look How Far" Review [Weekly, With Mentor]
Once a week, sit with your mentor and look at the full arc of progress so far. Not just today's work — the whole thing. Flip through the notebook. Look at the photo sequence. Count the marks on the chart. The purpose is to make the accumulation vivid. On a day-by-day basis, progress feels slow. On a week-by-week basis, it becomes undeniable.

### Practice 4: Mid-Project Check-In [At the Midpoint]
When the project reaches roughly its midpoint — half the rows planted, half the pieces assembled, half the pages drawn — stop and do a deliberate check-in with your mentor. Questions: How is it going? What has been hardest? Are you still interested? (Honest answer.) What will it take to finish? This is not a test. It is a moment to acknowledge the middle and to recommit to the finish.

### Practice 5: Stories of Finishers [Weekly — Read Aloud]
At least once a week, read aloud a story about someone who finished something that took a long time. A farmer who waited for the harvest. A builder who worked on a cathedral. An explorer who kept going when the journey seemed endless. After the story, discuss: How long did it take? What made them keep going? What would have happened if they had quit in the middle?

### Practice 6: Small Completions [Daily]
Continue the IC1.1-A1 practice of completing every task fully before starting the next one. This is the daily-scale version of what the long-term project practices at the multi-day scale. The muscle is the same: finish before you move on. Every completed chore, every cleaned-up activity, every finished assignment reinforces the identity of a person who completes things.

### Practice 7: The Waiting Garden [Optional but Recommended]
If you have any outdoor space at all, plant something. Even a single seed in a pot. Water it every day. Watch it grow. A garden is the most natural teacher of IC2.4 because the timeline is not yours to control. You cannot rush a plant. You can only show up, do the work, and wait. The patience a garden teaches transfers to every other long-term endeavor.

mentor_guide.formation_environment:
### Project Selection Is Critical

The most common Stretching Branches failure in IC2.4 is choosing the wrong project. If the project is too short, the child never encounters the middle. If it is too long, they encounter a middle that is genuinely beyond their developmental capacity to endure. If the project is not visible enough, the accumulation is invisible and the child cannot see that their effort is producing anything.

**Guidelines for project selection:**

- **Duration:** 2-4 weeks for first projects with younger students (ages 4-6). 4-8 weeks for older Stretching Branches students (ages 7-9) or for second and third projects.
- **Visibility:** The project must show visible progress after each work session. If the child cannot see that today's work added something, the project is wrong for this tier.
- **Physical reality:** Screen-based or abstract projects are not appropriate for Stretching Branches. The child needs to touch, see, and physically interact with something that is growing or being built.
- **Genuine interest:** The child must care about the project. Forcing a project the child has no interest in teaches compliance, not perseverance. Let the child choose from a curated set of options — you define the parameters, they choose within them.
- **Appropriate difficulty:** The project should be challenging enough to require sustained effort but not so difficult that the child cannot make meaningful progress in each session without adult intervention. They should be able to do most of the work themselves, with occasional help on hard parts.

**Good first projects by age:**

**Ages 4-5:**
- A simple garden: a few pots with seeds. Water daily. Watch growth. Harvest.
- A large drawing or painting built up over many sessions — adding details each time.
- A block or LEGO construction that gets more complex over days — but stays assembled between sessions.
- A collection: interesting rocks, leaves, or shells, gathered over walks, then sorted and displayed.

**Ages 6-7:**
- A garden with multiple plants and a simple layout.
- A model kit (appropriate complexity — not 1,000 pieces for a 6-year-old).
- A birdhouse or simple woodworking project with adult supervision on tools.
- A handmade book — written and illustrated over many sessions.

**Ages 8-9:**
- A vegetable garden from seed to harvest.
- A more complex building project — a shelf, a small piece of furniture, a fort.
- A detailed model (ship, airplane, building).
- A nature journal maintained over a season — observations, drawings, specimens.
- A multi-chapter story written and illustrated.

### Protecting the Work Sessions

The work sessions are non-negotiable. They are scheduled, they are consistent, and they happen whether the child feels like it or not. This is the fundamental lesson: effort is not governed by mood.

**Scheduling:**
- Set a regular time. "We work on your project every day after lunch" or "We work on your project Monday, Wednesday, and Friday after morning chores." The regularity matters more than the frequency.
- Minimum three sessions per week. Fewer than that, and the gap between sessions is too large — the child loses connection to the project.
- Session length should be age-appropriate: 15-20 minutes for ages 4-5, 20-30 minutes for ages 6-7, 30-45 minutes for ages 8-9. These can extend naturally if the child is engaged, but the minimum is the commitment.

**On days the child does not want to work:**
- They work anyway. Not for an extended session — even a shortened session of 10 minutes is acceptable on a hard day. But zero is not acceptable. The lesson of showing up when you do not feel like it is more important than the work done in any single session.
- Do not lecture about perseverance. Simply say: "It is time to work on your project." If they resist: "I know you do not feel like it today. We are going to do ten minutes. Let's see where you are after that." Often, starting is the hardest part — once they begin, they continue.

### Your Behavior in the Middle

The middle of the project is where your consistency matters most. The child will signal — sometimes explicitly, sometimes through avoidance — that they want to quit. Your response to these signals shapes whether the child develops perseverance or learns that difficulty is an exit condition.

**What the child may do in the middle:**
- Express boredom or disinterest. "This is boring." "I don't want to do this anymore."
- Propose starting a new project. "Can I build a rocket instead?"
- Reduce effort or quality. Rushing through sessions, doing sloppy work, trying to declare it "done" prematurely.
- Avoid the work sessions. "I forgot." "I'll do it later." "My stomach hurts."

**Your response:**
- Acknowledge the feeling without validating the escape. "I hear that you're tired of it. That is normal in the middle of a long project. Let's do today's session and see how you feel after."
- Do not negotiate on whether the session happens. The schedule holds.
- Do not add excitement artificially. Do not bribe or incentivize continuation. The reward is the completion itself. If you add external rewards, you teach the child that sustained effort requires external payment — a lesson that will cripple them later.
- Point to the progress chart. "Look at how much you have done. You are past the halfway point. The finish is closer than the start." Making the progress visible is your most powerful tool in the middle.
- Share your own experience. "I have felt that way about things I was working on. The middle is always the hardest part. It does not mean the project is wrong — it means you are in the middle."
mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Returns to the Project Without Coercion

**Developing well:** The child comes to the work session and begins without significant resistance. They may not be enthusiastic every time, but they start. On some days, they are genuinely engaged.

**Developing slowly:** Every session requires a battle. The child consistently resists starting and does not transition into engagement once begun.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the project genuinely interesting to the child? (If not, consider whether the project choice needs adjustment — not abandonment, but scope modification.) Are the sessions too long? (Shorten them.) Is the schedule inconsistent? (Fix the schedule before diagnosing the child.)

### 2. Tracks Progress Independently

**Developing well:** The child marks their progress chart or log after each session without being reminded. They reference the chart — looking back at it, counting entries, noting how far they have come.

**Developing slowly:** The mentor must remind the child to mark progress every time. The child does not reference the chart independently.

**How to improve:** Make the chart more prominent — at eye level, in the workspace. Try a different format (some children respond better to photos than to marks on paper). Involve the child in designing their own tracking method.

### 3. Completes a Full Project

**Developing well:** The child reaches the end of a multi-week project. The project is genuinely complete — not declared done prematurely, but finished to the standard that was set at the beginning.

**Developing slowly:** Projects are abandoned in the middle. The child reaches the hard phase and stops, either explicitly or through gradual disengagement.

**Critical note:** The first project may fail. If the child has never persisted through a multi-day project before, the first attempt may be genuinely too much. If this happens, do not treat it as a character failure. Adjust: choose a shorter project (one week instead of three), increase session frequency, and provide more presence during sessions. Build the capacity incrementally. But do not abandon the practice. The lesson that "I tried and it was too hard, so we stopped" is exactly the lesson IC2.4 exists to prevent.

### 4. Pushes Through the Middle

**Developing well:** The child expresses the normal feelings of the middle — boredom, desire to quit, wanting something new — but continues working. The feelings do not prevent the behavior.

**Developing slowly:** The feelings of the middle completely derail the work. The child cannot continue when interest fades.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the child getting support in the middle, or are they being left entirely alone with the difficulty? (Presence helps — not doing the work for them, but being there.) Is the child old enough for the project scope? (A 4-year-old's capacity for sustained effort is genuinely shorter than an 8-year-old's.) Are you, the mentor, modeling perseverance in your own projects? (The child who sees the adult quit when things get boring learns to quit.)

### 5. Does Not Replace Unfinished With New

**Developing well:** The child does not attempt to start new long-term projects when the current one is in progress. They may express desire for new projects, but they accept the rule: finish first, then begin.

**Developing slowly:** The child consistently lobbies for new projects when the current one enters the middle. The rule is fought repeatedly.

**Your response:** Hold the line. Calmly, consistently, without drama. "We finish this one first. When it is done, you can start the rocket. But this comes first." If you give in once — even once — the child learns that persistent lobbying works, and the rule is not real.

### 6. Participates in Completion Ritual

**Developing well:** The child is proud of the finished project. They want to show it. They participate in the completion ritual with genuine satisfaction.

**Developing slowly:** The child is indifferent to completion. They do not connect the finish to the effort that produced it.

**How to build this:** Make the ritual real. Do not rush past the finish. Take time to admire the work. Ask the child to narrate the process: "Tell me about this. When did you start? What was the hardest part? What are you most proud of?" The child needs to hear themselves tell the story of their perseverance.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Doing the Work for the Child
The project must be the child's work. If you are doing significant portions — assembling pieces, planting seeds, painting sections — the child is watching someone else persevere, not practicing it themselves. Help with things that are genuinely beyond the child's physical capability (operating power tools, reaching high places), but the bulk of the work must be theirs.

### Choosing Projects That Are Too Easy
A project that takes three days is not a long-term project. If the child never encounters the middle, they never practice persisting through it. The project must be long enough that the novelty wears off before the finish arrives.

### Choosing Projects That Are Too Hard
A project that is genuinely beyond the child's ability produces failure, not perseverance. If every session ends in frustration because the child cannot make meaningful progress, the project scope needs adjustment. The goal is successful persistence, not futile suffering.

### Inconsistent Session Schedule
If sessions happen "when we get around to it," the project will die. The schedule must be consistent. Missed sessions erode momentum and teach the child that the project is optional. If a session must be missed for genuine reasons, acknowledge it and make it up: "We missed yesterday because of the appointment. We will do an extra session tomorrow."

### Rescuing the Child From the Middle
When the child says "I want to quit," the temptation is to make it better — to add excitement, to modify the project into something easier, to let them take a long break. Resist. The middle is the lesson. Walking through it — not around it — is what builds perseverance. Your job is to hold steady, not to remove the difficulty.

### Excessive Praise for Routine Effort
As with IC1.1, do not over-praise normal compliance. Working on the project during the scheduled session is expected behavior, not heroic achievement. Save genuine recognition for genuine milestones: reaching the midpoint, pushing through a particularly hard session, completing the project. The daily practice should feel normal — because that is what you want it to become.

### Comparing Projects to Other Children's
Some children will produce more impressive projects than others. Some will work faster. The comparison that matters is internal: is this child's capacity for sustained effort growing compared to where they started? That is the only relevant measure.
mentor_guide.narration_evaluation:
### Prompt 1: Tell Me About Your Project
**Strong:** Detailed, shows ownership and awareness of the timeline. "I am building a birdhouse. I started two weeks ago. I have the walls done and the floor. I still need to do the roof and the perch." Knows where they are in the arc.
**Weak:** "I'm building something." No sense of progress, timeline, or what remains.

### Prompt 2: Hardest Part of Long-Term Work
**Strong:** Names the emotional challenge honestly. "The hardest part is when I don't feel like doing it but I have to anyway." Or: "The hardest part was the middle — I was bored and wanted to do something else."
**Weak:** "It's not hard." (Either the project is too easy, or the child is not yet self-aware enough to identify the struggle.)

### Prompt 3: Wanting to Quit
**Strong:** Admits it honestly and describes what happened. "Yes, I wanted to quit when I was gluing the side pieces because they kept falling. But I kept trying and figured out I needed to hold them longer."
**Weak:** "No." (If the project was genuinely challenging, the child experienced the desire to quit. Denying it suggests either the project was too easy or the child is not yet comfortable with honest self-examination.)

### Prompt 4: Progress Chart
**Strong:** Can point to specific entries and describe what they represent. Shows pride in the accumulation. "Look — this was the first day. I only did the base. And now I'm all the way here."
**Weak:** Cannot connect the marks to the work. Does not reference the chart independently.

### Prompt 5: Finishing Something Hard
**Strong:** Names the project, describes the difficulty and the duration, and expresses genuine satisfaction. The feeling of completion is real and articulated.
**Weak:** Cannot recall a completed long-term project, or describes something that was finished in a single session (not a long-term project).

### Prompt 6: Story of Perseverance
**Strong:** Recalls a specific person and specific circumstances. Can articulate what made them keep going and what would have been lost if they had stopped.
**Weak:** Generic response without specifics.
